did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study evaluated the SkinVision app (SVA), which uses an automated risk assessment (ARA) algorithm to analyze skin lesions for skin cancer detection. The paper explicitly mentions 'emerging deep learning algorithms and artificial intelligence networks' in the context of such apps and evaluates the app's automated analysis performance against dermatologist ratings.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental data from a multicentre study involving 125 participants and 199 lesions. It includes specific sections for Methods (study design, participants, statistical analysis), Results (interobserver agreement, weighted kappa), and Conclusions based on the collected data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Poor agreement between the automated risk assessment of a smartphone application for skin cancer detection and the rating by dermatologists
journal: Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of the SkinVision smartphone application's automated risk assessment (ARA) for skin cancer detection by comparing its ratings to those of dermatologists. The multicentre study enrolled 125 participants from the Dutch National Skin Cancer Day, who indicated 199 lesions they were concerned about. The app's automated ratings were compared against blinded dermatologist diagnoses, noting that lesions were not verified by histopathology. The results showed poor interobserver agreement between the app and dermatologists (weighted kappa = 0.02), with the app failing to analyze 45% of the lesions. Furthermore, a high proportion of lesions rated as high or medium risk by the app were diagnosed as benign by dermatologists. The authors concluded that the app's rating was poor and that further investigations are needed to assess its diagnostic accuracy in real-life scenarios.

all_task_types.details: The SkinVision app's automated risk assessment (ARA) predicts a discrete risk category (low, medium, or high risk) for skin lesions. The study compares these categorical ratings against dermatologist diagnoses (benign, atypical, malignant) and ratings (green, yellow, red). The output is a class label, not a continuous numeric value.

datasets_used.name: National Skin Cancer Day (Netherlands) Cohort
datasets_used.description: A multicentre study cohort consisting of participants from the Dutch National Skin Cancer Day who indicated lesions they were concerned about. Data was collected at four academic hospitals in the Netherlands (Leiden University Medical Center, Amsterdam Medical Center/VU University Medical Center, Maastricht University Medical Center, and Erasmus University Medical Center). Lesions were not verified by histopathology due to the setting.
classes_used: Automated Risk Assessment (ARA) ratings: Low (Green), Medium (Yellow), High (Red)
Dermatologist ratings: Green (Benign), Yellow (Atypical/Follow-up), Red (Malignant/Physician consult)

Dermatologist diagnoses: Benign naevus, Atypical naevus, Seborrhoeic keratosis, Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Bowen's disease, Solar lentigo, Lentigo maligna, Dermatofibroma, Angioma, Other benign, Other

use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the SkinVision app's automated risk assessment by comparing its ratings against blinded dermatologist diagnoses and ratings.
use.other: The dataset was used to assess interobserver agreement (weighted kappa) and the app's ability to analyze lesions (45% failure rate).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the interobserver agreement between the automated risk assessment (ARA) ratings and the dermatologist ratings.
To provide uncertainty estimates for the weighted kappa statistic.
To compare the characteristics of persons and lesions that could or could not be analysed by the SVA.
To describe the distribution of age.
To interpret the magnitude of the Kappa values.
To define the threshold for statistical significance.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the characteristics of persons and lesions that could or could not be analysed by the SkinVision app.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the characteristics of persons and lesions that could or could not be analysed by the SkinVision app.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was defined as P < 0.05. The study used the chi-square test to compare characteristics of participants and lesions that could or could not be analyzed by the app, finding a significantly higher proportion of unanalyzable lesions in the head/neck area.

National Skin Cancer Day (Netherlands) C.metric: Interobserver agreement (Weighted Kappa), Analysis Failure Rate, Diagnostic Accuracy of High/Medium Risk Ratings

SkinVision App (SVA) Automated Risk Asse.high_risk_false_positives: 67% of lesions rated high risk by app were diagnosed as benign naevus or seborrhoeic keratosis
SkinVision App (SVA) Automated Risk Asse.medium_risk_false_positives: 70% of lesions rated medium risk by app were diagnosed as benign naevus or seborrhoeic keratosis
SkinVision App (SVA) Automated Risk Asse.missed_malignancies: 9 BCC, 4 atypical naevi, 1 lentigo maligna were not analyzed by the app

SkinVision App (SVA) Automated Risk Asse.red_rating_dermatologist_agreement: 84% of ARA red ratings were rated green by dermatologist

National Skin Cancer Day (Netherlands) C.notes: The study found poor agreement between the app's automated risk assessment and dermatologist ratings. The app failed to analyze 45% of lesions, including several malignant or pre-malignant cases. A high proportion of lesions rated as high or medium risk by the app were actually benign. The study did not verify diagnoses with histopathology.

architecture_used.main_model: SkinVision App (SVA) automated risk assessment algorithm

architecture_details.model_family: Automated risk assessment algorithm (described as using 'emerging deep learning algorithms and artificial intelligence networks' in the discussion)

classification_or_output_head.details: The app provides an instant rating as low, medium, or high risk. The study notes that the app was unable to perform analysis in 45% of cases.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SkinVision App (SVA) automated risk assessment algorithm
SVA algorithm modification (referenced from Thissen et al.)
preprocessing_techniques: Image capture using the in-app automatic camera of the SkinVision app (SVA) on an iPhone
Multiple capture attempts (maximum of five) to acquire adequate images
Tagging of lesions as 'unable to perform analysis' if image capture was unsuccessful after five attempts
Blinding of dermatologists to the automated risk assessment results prior to rating and diagnosis
conclusions: The rating of the automated risk assessment (ARA) of the SkinVision smartphone application was poor, showing poor agreement with dermatologist ratings (weighted kappa = 0.02).
The application failed to analyze 45% of the lesions, including several malignant or pre-malignant cases such as basal cell carcinomas, atypical naevi, and lentigo maligna.
A high proportion of lesions rated as high or medium risk by the app were diagnosed as benign by dermatologists, indicating low specificity and high false-positive rates.

The study highlights the limitations of using automated risk assessment apps in unselected populations, as lesions were not verified by histopathology and were selected by participants rather than specialists.
Further investigations are needed to assess the diagnostic accuracy of such applications in real-life scenarios to provide consumers with reliable information.
important_remarks: Lesions were not verified by histopathology due to the legal constraints of the National Skin Cancer Day campaign setting, limiting the ability to confirm true diagnostic accuracy.
The app failed to analyze 45% of lesions (90/199), including 9 basal cell carcinomas, 4 atypical naevi, and 1 lentigo maligna, indicating significant safety risks for missed malignancies.
The study population exhibited selection bias, with 69% female participants and a median age of 50, which may not fully represent the general population or typical app users.
There was poor interobserver agreement between the app's automated risk assessment and dermatologist ratings (weighted kappa = 0.02; 95% CI -0.08 to 0.12; P = 0.74).
The app demonstrated low specificity, with 67% of lesions rated high risk and 70% rated medium risk by the app being diagnosed as benign (benign naevus or seborrhoeic keratosis) by dermatologists.
Different iPhone models were used across centers, though the authors noted this likely did not influence results as all supported the app's rating function.
The study highlights that CE certification only indicates technical compliance with EU health and safety requirements, not clinical efficacy or scientific validation.

The study design involved participants selecting their own lesions rather than specialists, providing a more realistic assessment of performance in unselected, real-life scenarios compared to previous studies.
